2024 · NeurIPS 2024 · Benchmarks & Evals

A Careful Examination of Large Language Model Performance on Grade School Arithmetic

Is GSM8k measuring math reasoning, or memorization? Scale AI hand-builds GSM1k — 1,205 brand-new grade-school problems engineered to match GSM8k's difficulty distribution — keeps it private, and re-tests every major model. The worst overfitters drop up to 8 points; frontier models barely move; and the gap tracks how likely a model is to spit GSM8k back out verbatim.

Hugh Zhang, Jeff Da, Dean Lee, Vaughn Robinson, Catherine Wu, Will Song, … Russell Kaplan, Michele Lunati, Summer Yue · Scale AI
venue NeurIPS 2024 D&B citations 204 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06-21
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

The field steers itself by public benchmarks — GSM8k for grade-school math, MATH, MBPP, HumanEval, SWE-bench. But every modern LLM is trained on a giant scrape of the internet, and those benchmarks live on that internet. If questions closely resembling the test set leak into pretraining or fine-tuning, a high score can reflect memorization rather than reasoning, and the yardstick quietly stops measuring what we think it measures.

By mid-2024 this suspicion was everywhere but rigorously unproven. You can't inspect a proprietary training set, and the standard defenses — n-gram overlap filters, embedding-similarity decontamination — are leaky and impossible to audit from outside. GSM8k in particular had become the gold standard for elementary arithmetic, with top models reporting >95% — exactly the regime where a few stolen points decide bragging rights.

So the authors run the clean experiment the field couldn't: build a fresh twin of GSM8k that is provably absent from any training set, match it to GSM8k as tightly as humanly possible, and measure the gap. The move is borrowed straight from a celebrated 2019 study (Recht et al.) that rebuilt CIFAR-10 and ImageNet test sets to measure overfitting in image classifiers — here applied to LLM reasoning.

// the counterfactual they approximate

What would each model score if GSM8k's test set had never been published? GSM1k is the closest observable proxy: same style, same difficulty, but guaranteed unseen. The GSM8k→GSM1k drop is the overfitting that public release bought.

// how it works

The method

GSM1k is 1,205 problems requiring only elementary arithmetic (+ − × ÷) with positive-integer answers — built entirely by human annotators, with no LLM or synthetic data in the loop. That constraint is load-bearing: a synthetically-generated twin could itself be contaminated by the model it came from, so every problem is written from scratch by a person shown three GSM8k examples of a target difficulty and asked to produce a novel problem of the same complexity.

The hard part isn't writing new problems — it's making them distributionally identical to GSM8k so any score drop can't be blamed on GSM1k simply being harder. The authors control for difficulty on four axes and then validate the match with three independent checks:

  • Solution-step distribution. Difficulty is approximated by the number of reasoning steps (counted from "calculator" tags in solutions); the requested distribution mirrors GSM8k's.
  • Answer magnitude. A final winnowing pass discards candidate problems until GSM1k's answer-magnitude CDF matches GSM8k's (LLMs are known to fail on larger numbers, so this removes a confound) — see Figure 1.
  • Human distinguishability. Shown five problems (four GSM8k, one GSM1k) and paid to spot the odd one out, 19 fresh annotators succeeded just 21.83% of the time — barely above the 20% chance rate.
  • Human solve rate. Under a 15-minute timer, annotators solved 4.07 ± 0.93 GSM8k problems vs 4.36 ± 1.11 GSM1k problems — i.e. GSM1k is comparable, if anything slightly easier.

Quality control is a three-layer gauntlet: every problem is reviewed by trusted annotators, then independently re-solved blind (mismatched answers are discarded), then audited by Scale's general QA team. Of 2,108 initial problems, 1,419 survived the blind re-solve and 1,375 passed audit, leaving 1,205 after the magnitude match.

Evaluation & the memorization probe

Every model is run through a fork of EleutherAI's LM Evaluation Harness with one identical setting — 5-shot prompts drawn from the GSM8k train set, temperature 0, max generation raised from 256 to 1,000 tokens so chains of thought don't truncate. Because the harness is held constant rather than tuned per model, reported GSM8k numbers can sit below a model's official figure; the object of study is the GSM8k − GSM1k gap, not the absolute score.

To ask why a model overfits, they probe for memorization directly on open-weight models: the per-character-normalized log-likelihood of generating the GSM8k test set, used as a proxy for "has this sequence been seen in training."

L(x) = 1c Σi log p(xi | x<i) // c = characters in the sequence (normalizes across tokenizers); higher L ⇒ the model finds GSM8k more "natural" to generate

Crucially, GSM1k is not released publicly — releasing it would recreate the very contamination it diagnoses. The authors hold out an extra vetted set as a leak tripwire and precommit to open-sourcing everything once either three open models from distinct lineages clear 95% on GSM1k, or June 2025 arrives — whichever comes first.

// what they found

The results

Contamination is real but bounded. The worst-overfit model loses 8.0 points moving to the clean set (Yi-6B-Chat, 43.7% → 35.7%), and entire model families — Phi and Mistral above all, with Yi, Xwin, Gemma, and CodeLlama trailing — overfit consistently across nearly every size and version. Examining a whole family rather than one checkpoint is what licenses the word "systematic."

The flip side is the reassuring half: frontier models show essentially no overfitting. GPT-4o, GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4, the Claude 3 family, Gemini 1.5, and even proprietary Mistral Large all perform within noise on GSM1k — and many land above the line of no overfit, consistent with GSM1k being marginally easier. Mistral Large being the only un-overfit member of an otherwise-overfit family is a tell: the simplest explanation isn't that Mistral specially decontaminated their biggest model, but that sufficiently strong models genuinely learn arithmetic and generalize even when GSM8k sat in their training data.

ModelGSM8kGSM1kΔ on GSM1k
Yi-6B-Chat43.735.7−8.0
math-shepherd-mistral-7b-rl82.675.4−7.2
Xwin-Math-13B66.059.6−6.4
phi-256.650.4−6.3
Llama-3-8B-Instruct75.269.0−6.2
GPT-4o93.192.9−0.2
Mistral Large85.385.4+0.1
GPT-491.192.3+1.2
Claude 3 Opus80.282.4+2.2
Gemini 1.5 Flash79.783.5+3.8

Overfit doesn't mean it can't reason

A natural fear is that overfit models are pure lookup tables. The data refutes this. Phi-2 drops 6.3 points yet still solves over half of GSM1k — problems guaranteed absent from its training set — matching Llama-2-70B, a model 25× larger. Even the most contaminated models are doing real reasoning; their benchmark numbers are inflated, not fabricated. (Separately, several obscure models topping the Open LLM Leaderboard crater on GSM1k — a clean demonstration of Goodhart's law, gaming the metric rather than improving the skill.)

Memorization explains part of it — not all

Is leakage the cause? Partly. Across open models there's a positive correlation between how readily a model generates the GSM8k test set and how much it overfits: Spearman's r² = 0.36 (p = 0.03), with each point of GSM8k→GSM1k gap associated with a 1.2×10⁻² rise in per-character log-likelihood (Pearson r² = 0.26, Kendall's τ = 0.29). But the outliers are the interesting part. Math-Shepherd-Mistral-7B-RL is heavily overfit yet has low log-likelihood on GSM8k — its process reward model likely leaked correct reasoning chains without the problems themselves ever appearing. Conversely Llemma has high log-likelihood but minimal overfit; its open training data is known to contain only a handful of GSM8k examples, too few to distort it. So overfitting can arrive by indirect routes — collecting benchmark-like data, or selecting checkpoints on benchmark scores — with no literal test-set leak at all.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

This is an unusually careful piece of measurement, and its core design is right: comparing GSM8k to GSM1k inside one fixed harness cancels most confounds, so the gap is a credible overfitting signal even though the absolute GSM8k numbers run below official leaderboard figures. The honesty about that deflation — and the manual-answer-extraction and alternative-prompt ablations (Appendices J and E) showing the trends survive — is exactly what you want.

The seams are in how much weight any single model's gap can bear. "Overfit" is one scalar, and it is prompt-sensitive: deepseek-math-7b-rl looks un-overfit under the standard prompt (it actually does better on GSM1k) but shows a +6.4-point drop under the alternative prompt — the sign flips. The authors know this, which is precisely why the strong claims are pinned to family-level consistency and the log-likelihood correlation rather than to individual checkpoints. Read per-model deltas as noisy; read the family patterns as the result.

baselines

The right comparison (same harness, same 5-shot GSM8k prompt across all models) and the honest one (absolute scores intentionally not maximized per model). Ablations on answer extraction and prompt format confirm the overfitting trend is robust, even as exact numbers move.

contamination

The cleanest part: GSM1k is human-written, never released, with a held-out tripwire set. The residual risk is self-flagged — proprietary models had to be evaluated via API, trusting providers not to train on the submitted questions.

what's missed

One benchmark, grade-school arithmetic, already near saturation (its own June-2025 release trigger concedes the skill is becoming undiscriminating). Many per-model gaps aren't individually significant (e.g. GPT-4o p≈0.41); the load is carried by aggregate patterns. And since GSM1k is, by the human solve-rate check, slightly easier, true overfitting is plausibly understated. The memorization probe (Fig 4) only works on open weights — closed models can't be mechanistically explained.

would raise confidence

Per-model significance with multiple-comparison correction; extending the private-precommitted-twin recipe to harder benchmarks (MATH, GPQA, code); logprob access for closed models; and the eventual public release (which landed in 2025) enabling independent replication.

// bottom line

A disciplined refutation-and-confirmation: benchmark contamination is real and measurable for specific families, but frontier math performance is mostly genuine reasoning, and memorization is only one of several routes to an inflated score. The lasting contribution is less the 2024 leaderboard than the methodology — a human-authored, difficulty-matched, deliberately-withheld twin benchmark with a precommitted release — a template the contamination-resistant eval line (LiveBench, Search-Time Contamination, SWE-Bench Pro) has been building on since.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. The contamination worry is real but bounded. Worst families (Phi, Mistral, Yi, Xwin) drop up to 8 points on a fresh twin of GSM8k; frontier models (GPT-4o, GPT-4, Claude 3, Gemini 1.5, Mistral Large) are flat or better — strong models learn arithmetic, they don't just memorize it.
  2. Overfit ≠ inability to reason. Even the most contaminated models solve novel problems — Phi-2 clears half of GSM1k, matching a 25×-larger Llama-2-70B. Benchmark scores are inflated, not fabricated.
  3. Memorization is part of the story (Spearman r² = 0.36 between GSM8k log-likelihood and the gap), not all of it: benchmark-similar training data, checkpoint selection, and leaked reasoning chains can overfit a model the test set never literally touched.
  4. The real deliverable is a recipe: hand-build a distribution-matched twin of a benchmark, keep it private, precommit to release. That's how you measure overfitting without manufacturing the next leak.
